sentences.text: Yes.
Steven Samuel Mendelsson.
No.
I was-- my name at birth was Stefan, S-T-E-F-A-N.
M-E-N-D-E-L-double S-O-N.
Seventh of May, 1926.
I was born in Breslau in Germany.
Today that city is in Poland.
East of the Oder-Neisse Line it is called Wroclaw.
I'm now getting on for 71.
Yes.
I was born into what you can possibly call a middle-- middle class type of family.
My father was a businessman.
And my parents were blessed or otherwise with two sons.
I was the older one.
My younger brother Walter was born exactly four years later, in May 1930.
We grew up I would say in a fairly cultural sort of family background.
My father played the violin.
My mother was very good at the piano.
And they used to make a lot of music between them.
They used to have a lot of parties at which a lot of music was being played in the house.
And very often my parents used to go to Vienna in order to attend the state opera or any of the other social functions there.
Vienna perhaps because my father's sister and her husband and son lived there, and so they had some sort of a contact over there.
The Jewish life was not terribly exposed in any way.
Certainly we were Jewish.
My parents, my grandfather used to go to synagogue on the high holidays.
But we were not what is known as shomrei Shabbat.
We did not observe the Sabbath as such.
And we were obviously-- most of the friends, most of my parents friends were, I should imagine, Jewish.
And ultimately, I went to a Jewish school.
And so I acquired, of course, a lot of Jewish friends.

And as, in fact, the danger of Nazis, and especially in 1933 when Hitler came to power, became fairly obvious, then we in fact became more Jewish-minded as well, because we obviously became a-- a member, or we became a family of a minority, of an oppressed minority.
As far as I know, my parents, yes, and my grandparents were always members of the synagogue.
They must have paid their dues, I'm quite convinced.
And as such, we lived a very normal sort of civil type of life.
The-- my parents belonged to a very large synagogue in Breslau, but--but without any doubt the largest of its kind in the city.
It was huge.
I think it accommodated something like 2 to 3,000 congregants.
And I do remember on the high holidays that that synagogue was chock-a-block full.
It always impressed me.

It was in the middle of town, fairly close to a police headquarters, in fact, next door to a police headquarters, and not too far away from the royal residence where Prussian kings used to live when they came to the city.

And it was one of the main buildings in Breslau, in so far that whenever you went for a walk around the town or in the town, wherever there was an opportunity to see the skyline of the city, invariably the new synagogue as it was called, the [NON-ENGLISH] was invariably indicated.
So there was the town hall and there was a [NON-ENGLISH], a special building that was built in Breslau to celebrate a century of whatever it was.
I really don't know.
And the new synagogue, together with also the cathedral and one or two other prominent churches, always figured on those plates.
And I remember about seeing that even in 1900 and I would say 36, and perhaps even in 1937.
It was always a-- a-- a major monument in the town.
Oh, yes.
During the Kristallnacht, which occurred on the 9th of November 1938, then the synagogue, like all other synagogues, was obviously destroyed.
They pumped, as far as I understand, tremendous numbers of gallons of petrol into the building in order to set it alight.
It had a very large cupola as its centerpiece.
And on top of the cupola was a very large Magen David, the star of David, that possibly was made from lead or something.
And as in fact the roof started to crumble, so this Magen David fell, ironically enough, onto a wing of the police headquarters which had been taken over by the Gestapo in those days.
And it is said that 13 of their officials were killed by the fall of that huge Magen David onto the police headquarters, yes.
I understand that on Simchat Torah they managed to exhibit 156 holy scrolls, which they paraded around the bima.
So it was a very large synagogue, yes.
Oh, yes.
I would say very much a more German life.
They never denied the fact that they were Jewish.
They were proud of it.
But I should imagine they perhaps belonged to as many German societies, clubs, or organizations as they did to Jewish ones.
I do not know.
I was 12 years old when I left home.
And so I don't really remember exact details of my parents' activities.
Oh, yes.
My father, I understand, served in the Imperial German forces in the First World War.
And he was in fact-- most of his time was in Eastern-- in fact, extensively in Eastern Europe, particularly on the Russian front.
And he served there as an expert in logistics and transportation, because such was the nature of my-- the business that my grandfather had founded, a business of forwarding agents, as it were.
That means that they-- they transported goods from one place to another to their specific destinations.
They had-- the headquarters of the company was in Breslau and, oddly enough, within the old original ghetto.
But that was just a coincidence I should imagine certainly.
And they had a branch in Szczecin, which was at the mouth of the Odra into the Baltic Sea.
And then they had an agency in Chicago, from where they imported a tremendous amount of meat into Germany, into-- into Breslau via ships, of course, in those days.
And Breslau was a major city for distribution.
It was the gateway, if you like, to Eastern Europe.
It was a city of about half a million inhabitants and played a major part both as a traffic center, you can call it that, mainly rail, of course.
Roads in those days were not quite as-- as functional as they are today.
And they distributed goods that had been bought by other organizations to Eastern Europe, certainly to Prague, to Poland, and as far perhaps as the Ukraine and White Russia.
Yes, indeed.
Yes.
My grandfather's name was Samuel.
And my father's name was Franz.
My mother was called Margereta.
And I was named Stefan.
And the emphasis there is put on the first letter S, because it was intended.
My parents intended this, my grandparents, that I would ultimately follow them into the business.
And since the company that my grandfather founded was called S.
Mendelsson and Son, it was going to be simple for me ultimately to also become an S.
Mendelsson to take over the company.
But that was not to happen.
No.
That was, of course, not to happen.
The company, like all other organizations, Jewish organizations, were totally destroyed, confiscated.
And so I in fact left Germany and was never in a position to take up that position.
Yes.
I went to school.
I think I must have been 5 or 6 when I started school.
And I went to a local school, a primary school, elementary-- I suppose we call it a primary school, junior school.
And there, we had about 30 children in the class, and amongst them were 6 or 7 Jewish children.
And one of the teachers was a particularly-- I wouldn't like to say pro-Jewish, but certainly was very tolerant towards Jewish children.
I remember an incidence when he had announced that he was going to show in the geography lesson-- he was going to show some slides.
In those days, we're talking about 1933, '34, '35, that was still quite something fairly advanced and unusual.
And we were all looking forward to this.
And there was one German girl who sat behind me and said, oh, goody. goody.
That means that when you're going to show the slides on the announced date, the Jewish kids won't see it because of it will be Yom Kippur and they won't be here.
And when he heard this, he changed the date of the showing of the slides.
And it was very, very sad, but four weeks later or so, he was removed from his position and I do not know what has happened to him.
But he was obviously too tolerant or too friendly towards the Jews.
And that didn't fit in quite with the Nazi regime.
And that must have been I suppose in 1934, '35.
No, not really.
I was aware of the Hitler Youth.
I was aware of Nazi organizations.
We saw them parading up and down the streets very often.
I'd been warned by my parents to keep clear of them, not to interfere, not to get involved in anything.
But on occasions I was beaten up by gangs of Hitler Youth boys who were more or less my age, perhaps a year old, perhaps a year younger.
They usually operated and marauded in gangs of three or four.
And it was difficult as one against four to hold one's own.
But quite clearly, I did my best and defended myself, [LAUGHS] and as you can see, I'm still here to tell the tale.
After I finished the so-called Volksschule, which was the sort of junior school, I then graduated to a grammar school, a gymnasium.
And since Breslau had a fairly large Jewish community of about 21 or 22,000 all together, they had two Jewish schools.
One was attached or was next to the synagogue that I mentioned to you early on.
And the other one was a-- a-- a-- a-- grammar school to which I went.
And it was at that grammar school that whenever we came out of school, almost invariably there were hordes of Hitler Youth boys waiting for us.
And they set upon us and doffled us up, as it were.
So that-- by that time, we were very, very conscious of anti-Semitism, yes.
We were not allowed to go to sports places other than those that had in fact been accredited specifically for Jews.
It was almost like apartheid as we knew it in South Africa.
[GERMAN] and that's all right, you know.
There was water there.
We could swim.
But we were not allowed to go any public swimming places or cinemas.
Well, at that time-- I left Germany in June-- no.
I'm sorry.
I left Germany in March 1939.
And that-- at the age of 12, just coming on for 13.
And that would be-- at the grammar school, I was there for three full years.
And that would be from 10 to 13 or 10 to 12 and 3/4, something--
Yes, of course.
They were looking around.

But like so many other Jews in Germany, who were very, very much assimilated and considered themselves firstly Germans of Mosaic persuasion-- they never denied the fact that they were Jewish, but their German nationality seemed to be predominant, as it were.
Rather than saying we were Jews who happened to have German nationality, they were Germans who happened to be of Jewish religion.
Yes, they tried to some extent-- and this is all hearsay from my mother.
I was obviously as a little child not involved in this.
They tried to emigrate to various places.
And every country had its quota.
Every country had its restrictions.

I remember, for instance, had we wanted to go to France, they would've accepted us with open arms, because my parents only had two boys and France having lost so much of her manhood in the First World War, '14, '18, there were only too placed-- too pleased to replenish the young-- young males amongst their midst.
For some reason or other, this didn't materialize.

And it wasn't until my father was taken into concentration camp in Buchenwald that my mother, who was then left alone, as it were, as far as our family was concerned, actually started working very, very hard and trying to get out.
And we did obtain an affidavit, I understand, to enter the United States by 1943.
Now, don't forget, we are still now in 1939, so that meant another four years of hell, of possible total annihilation.
One didn't know what was likely to happen.
And so the situation became fairly, fairly dicey.
Yes.
The amazing thing is we lived in a very large house.
And after, after, after possibly 1937 or '38, my-- our family, my father, mother, brother, and myself, moved into this particular house that was property of my grandfather.
Families tended to sort of close up a little bit more.
It gave them moral support.
It was economically sound.
And so we lived in this very big house.
And the house was taken care of by a caretaker, who also had an accommodation in this house in the basement.
He, his wife, and two sons were living there.
And they were not Jewish.

And during the atrocities of the destruction of the synagogues and Jewish shops that started at the Kristallnacht, round about 12 o'clock midnight on that very day or at the end of that very day, the Gestapo came in order to collect my father and my grandfather to be carted off to Buchenwald.
And this caretaker, who was a member of the Nazi party, opened the gates and told them that they had already been collected.
Yes, indeed.
And that literally saved them from-- and so the Nazis went away, trusting the information to be correct.
And so they-- they managed to stay at home for the time being.
Yes.
His name was Sacher.
I don't know his first name.
You know, in Germany everything, especially children vis-a-vis adults, was always by, by the family name.
So he was known as Herr Sacher.
And he had a wife.
They were pleasant people and they had two sons.
And they were roughly of my brother's and my age.
And we often played with them.
Early on, in the early days, we used to play in the garden and perhaps even on the street.
Later on, the thing became a little bit more dicey and we played either in our abode with the boys, or sometimes we went downstairs to their abode.
And I suppose on reflection that was at the request of Mr.
Sacher, who possibly didn't want others to see that his boys were playing with Jewish children.
So that seemed to make some sense, anyway.
And my father and grandfather then remained in the house.
They obviously didn't go to work.
They didn't leave the house during the Kristallnacht [GERMAN] operation.
Yes, if you like, in their own house, always worried that the Nazis may come back again.

I think it's perhaps interesting to note that on the very first day, on the morning of all this activity, a lady who used to come-- non-Jews, who used to come and help in the house, was sort of a cleaner, if you like-- phoned up very early in the morning my mother and said, whatever you do, do not send the children to school today.
And when my mother asked why, she said, well, just don't send them.
And then she hang up.
And perhaps two or three hours later-- we were, of course, kept at home-- opposite our house lived another Jewish family.
And I witnessed through our window how a police van that was being manned by Gestapo or Nazi SS people rushed into the house and in fact dragged out the owner and his wife.
It was fairly early in the morning, about 8 o'clock or so.
She was still in her dressing gown.
And they just grabbed hold of this man and dragged him down the stairs and bunged him into this Black Maria.
And the wife was trying to sort of hold back, tried to pull him back.
And they just kicked her out of the way and she fell to the ground.
That is a good question.
I don't remember that.
Yes.
I, I thought-- I thought it was quite frightening.
I knew the people well.
They were pleasant people.
They were kind to us.
They were, I suppose, kind to all other people as well.
And I thought this was a dreadful thing to do.
After about two days, on the 11th or 12th of November-- I forget the exact date-- a message came over the radio that Dr.
Goebbels had decided to call off the-- call off the [NON-ENGLISH], the operation, the rounding up of male Jews.
And so my father ventured out in order to go to the business to see what happened.
We didn't have a car.
This was a terrific luxury in those days.
And so he went by tram.
But on the way to the tram-- that was a fairly extensive tram service, the [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH] in Breslau-- he was suddenly apprehended by a Gestapo patrol.
And they bunged him into their van or car or what.
And for about a week, we heard nothing from him.
We, of course, feared the worst.
And then we got a postcard from him saying he is well and well looked after.
And he is in a rest home, or something to that effect.
And the postmark was Buchenwald, which we knew, of course, what sort of a rest home that was.
Yes.
And after about another two or three days, my mother received a postcard from him, again his handwriting.
Would you please send some warm clothes, because it was now getting towards Christmas.
It was winter.
Winters were very, very cold out in German--
Yeah.
And he needed warm clothes, because the only clothes he had were those that he left the house with in order to go and have a look at this business.
And my mother then purchased some very expensive and very warm coats and underwear and shirts and socks and so on, so forth.
And the Germans were always very thorough.
They acknowledged the receipt of-- of this particular parcel and thanked my mother for her donation towards needy Germans, part of the psychological warfare.
So we knew that that had gone to someone else.
Good luck to them.
But my father was, of course, left without it.
And that in itself was a considerable worry in the mind what, in fact, was likely to happen to him and so on.
They were tough days.

And early on in 1939, the first or second week in January or so, Goring, Field Marshal Goring, made an announcement whereby all those Jews who were in concentration camps and who had fought in the First World War with the Imperial German forces would in fact be released.
Right.
Thank you.
